coredata.dc:title: Genistein carbon dots exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, Kumar Sagar
Malka, Orit
Shauloff, Nitzan
Bersudsky, Marina
Voronov, Elena
Gupta, Bhawna
Jelinek, Raz
coredata.dc:description: Genistein, an isoflavone from soybean, has attracted attention due to its health benefits, particularly antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Clinical applications of genistein, however, have been limited due to the considerable hydrophobicity and lower bioavailability of the molecule. In this study, carbon dots (C-dots) synthesized from genistein as the carbonaceous precursor exhibit antioxidant properties in test-tube and cell experiments. Anti-inflammatory activity of the genistein-C-dots was also recorded in LPS stimulated macrophages, manifested in inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokine levels and enhancement anti-inflammatory cytokine expression. The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of the genistein-C-dots, particularly in comparison to the parent genistein molecules, likely account to the display of functional genistein residues on the C-dots’ surfaces, and low band gap energy facilitating electron scavenging. Importantly, the genistein-C-dots featured biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, underlining their potential as a therapeutic vehicle against inflammatory conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Genistein
Carbon dots
Isoflavone
Phytochemicals
Antioxidants
Anti-inflammation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protective effect of genistein-C-dots against ROS/RNS in cells
Cell uptake and biocompatibility of genistein-C-dots
Effect of genistein-C-dots on cellular cytokine levels

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Kumar Sagar Jaiswal [Ref No. 3/1/2(21)/OBS/2019-NCD–II ] is thankful to Indian Council of Medical Research [ICMR], India for financial support and Council of Higher Education (CHE), Israel for infrastructural support.
We are grateful to the Israel Science Foundation for generous support (grant number 337/20 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of the genistein-carbon dots and their biological properties. A. The simple synthesis scheme for production of genistein-C-dots. B. AFM image of the genistein-C-dots. C. FTIR spectra of the genistein-C-dots and genistein precursor molecules. Flavone functional groups (∼1650 cm−1) and C-O-C (∼1040 cm−1) functional moieties of genistein are indicated by the arrows. D. Contact angles of genistein-C-dots and genistein precursor; Pronounced hydrophilicity of genistein-C-dots is recorded.

Antioxidant activities of genistein-carbon dots. A. DPPH free radical scavenging assay; B. KMnO4 reduction assay. Both assays shown in panels A and B demonstrate greater antioxidant activities of genistein-C-dots compared to genistein. C. Change in fluorescence emission intensity of the genistein-C-dots (125 µg/ml) after incubation with different ROS and RNS (300 µM). D. Band gap energy (Eg, evaluated using the Kubelka-Munk function). Antioxidant assays were performed in three independent experiments, where each experiment was performed in triplicates. Data are shown as mean ± SD.

Cell uptake and toxicity of genistein carbon dots. A. Uptake of Genistein-C-dots by RAW 264.7 macrophages (Scale bars correspond to 20 µm). Hoechst dye excitation was 405 nm and C-dot excitation was 488 nm. B. C-dot cytotoxicity in the case of RAW 264.7 macrophages. C. Hemolysis assay.

Intracellular antioxidant activities of genistein carbon dots. A. DCFDA assay for intracellular ROS; B. Griess assay to estimate RNS. The experiments utilized LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages. Data recorded in three independent experiments, where each experiment was performed in triplicate. Data are shown as mean ± SD. Green bars and red bars represent results for genistein-C-dots and genistein, respectively. Statistical significance was calculated with One-way ANOVA and is shown as: * p < 0.05, * * p < 0.01.

Anti-inflammatory properties of genistein carbon dots. Extracellular levels of pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages: A. TNF-α; B IL-6; C. IL-10. Data are shown as mean ± SD. Statistical significance using One-way ANOVA is indicated, * < 0.05, * * p < 0.01.

head.ce:title: Genistein carbon dots exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in vitro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Genistein, an isoflavone from soybean, has attracted attention due to its health benefits, particularly antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Clinical applications of genistein, however, have been limited due to the considerable hydrophobicity and lower bioavailability of the molecule. In this study, carbon dots (C-dots) synthesized from genistein as the carbonaceous precursor exhibit antioxidant properties in test-tube and cell experiments. Anti-inflammatory activity of the genistein-C-dots was also recorded in LPS stimulated macrophages, manifested in inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokine levels and enhancement anti-inflammatory cytokine expression. The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of the genistein-C-dots, particularly in comparison to the parent genistein molecules, likely account to the display of functional genistein residues on the C-dots’ surfaces, and low band gap energy facilitating electron scavenging. Importantly, the genistein-C-dots featured biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, underlining their potential as a therapeutic vehicle against inflammatory conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammation is one of the most critical aspects of host immune system in providing defense against tissue injuries, infectious pathogens and immunological reactions [1]. Moreover, aberrant regulation of inflammation might result in chronic diseases such as diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and others, presenting major health risks [1,2]. In general, chronic inflammation is associated with a number of factors including hyperactivation of immune cells enhanced release of inflammatory cytokines, lipid peroxidation, metabolic stress and generation of free radicals [3]. Production of radicals, specifically reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), exert particular damage in inflammation [4].

A plethora of therapeutic strategies have been developed to counter inflammatory conditions, primarily steroidal and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) [5]. However, most anti-inflammatory drugs induce side effects. Alternative antioxidant agents which aid in treatment of inflammatory conditions, such as enzyme mimetics (SOD/SOD-catalase, glutathione peroxidases), vitamins (C and E), dietary supplements (N-acetylcysteine) and phytochemicals have also faced barriers mostly due to insufficient delivery to intracellular targets and inability to target organelles responsible for ROS production [6,7].

Plants contain various phytochemicals constituting useful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agents [8,9]. Plants are rich in secondary metabolites such as flavonoids, glycosides, tannins, alkaloids and terpenoids, some of which have therapeutic potential [10]. Phytochemicals are considered particularly as an important source for anti-inflammatory therapeutics [11]. Genistein is an isoflavone extracted from various legumes [12] and exhibits health benefits [13], including anti-inflammatory, anti-obesity, and anti-cancer effects [11]. Importantly, genistein has been most effective in downregulating cytokine induced signaling cascades and is considered a potent anti-inflammatory agent [12,14]. Genistein, however, has practical limitations as a therapeutic agent including low water solubility and limited bioavailability [15]. To overcome these difficulties researchers have pursued varied strategies, including substitution of hydroxyl groups of genistein [16], embedding the molecule in micro-micelles [17], or encapsulated within nanoparticles [18].

Carbon dots (C-dots), < 10 nm carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles, have emerged in recent years as a unique, versatile class of advanced carbon nanomaterials [19]. C-dots exhibit chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and cell permeability, and have been utilized in varied biological applications [20–22]. C-dots have been employed as drug delivery vehicles primarily owing to their bioavailability [23], molecular targeting capabilities [24], and solubility in aqueous media [25]. Natural compounds including plant substances [26], fruit peels [27], vegetables [28], and phytochemicals [29,30] have been utilized as precursors for C-dot synthesis. Importantly, due to the mild synthesis conditions, particularly relatively low preparation temperatures, C-dot generally retain on their surface functional moieties from their carbonaceous precursors, facilitating molecular targeting [31].

Here, we report on the synthesis of C-dots from genistein as the primary carbonaceous building block, retaining the functional units of the parent compound. The genistein-C-dots were tested for their biocompatibility, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory effects. The biological activities of genistein-C-dots were found to be superior to the individual genistein molecules in in-vitro inflammation model of LPS stimulated macrophages. This study underscores the therapeutic potential of phytochemical-based C-dots in inflammation conditions.

Potassium ferricyanide, trichloroacteic acid, ferric chloride, hydrogen peroxide, sodium hydroxide and dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), sulfanilamide, N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrchloride, sodium nitrite, phosphoric acid, sodium nitrate, lipopolysaccharide from Pseudomonas aeruginosa 10, glutathione (GSH), sodium hypochlorite, ethylenediamine and benzoylated dialysis membrane (2KDa) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA. Genistein (purity>98%) was purchased from TCI Europe. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and 2-mercaptoethanol, Pen-Strep solution (Penicillin-10000 units/ml and Streptomycin 10 mg/ml), sodium bicarbonates and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were acquired from Biological Industries, USA. Cell viability reagent 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and Hoechst 33258 were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA. DCFDA - Cellular ROS Assay Kit and Griess reagents were acquired from Abcam, USA. ELISA Kits for cytokines were obtained from Peprotech, Israel. Deionized water with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C were used for all the experiments.

Synthesis of C-dots was carried out according to previously published methods with slight modifications [31]. Genistein (300 mg) and equal volume of ethyelenediamine (300 mg) were dissolved in 0.05 M NaOH solution and bath-sonicated at 37 kHz frequency for 15 min until completely solubilized. The mixture was poured into a 20 ml Teflon hydrothermal reactor autoclave and heated in oven to 180°C for 8 h. Once reaction was completed, brown colored solution was obtained confirming the formation of C-dots. The resultant solution was filtered with 0.22 µM syringe filter and transferred for dialysis in deionized water for 24 h. The dialyzed solution was collected and freeze-dried to obtain genistein-C-dot powder. The solid powder was stored at 4°C until further use.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was applied in tapping mode using AC160 TS probe (Cypher-ES, Oxford Instrument). The samples for AFM were prepared by drop casting the aqueous solution of genestein-C-dots on freshly cleaved mica surface followed by drying at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out using a Nicolet FTIR spectrometer (6700 FTIR spectrometer) and data analysis was carried out by the OMNIC program. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer ESCALAB 250 ultrahigh vacuum (1 *10−9 bar) instrument coupled with an Al Kα X-ray source and a monochromator. The X-ray beam size was kept at 500 µm and survey spectra was recorded with pass energy (PE) 150 eV and high energy resolution spectra were recorded with pass energy (PE) 20 eV. XPS results were processed with AVANTGE program. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorbance spectra were recorded with a Thermo Scientific Evolution 220 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy experiments were carried out on a Flourolog spectrofluorimeter, Horiba. Quantum yield (QY) of genistein-C-dots was measured using a QuantaPhi-2 integrating sphere coupled with Flourolog spectrofluorimeter, Horiba. Ultraviolet–Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy analysis was done on a Cary 5000 + UMA, Agilent. Hydrophobicity of the samples were measured through contact angle meter, Attension Theta Lite, Biolin Scientific.

The ability of genistein-C-dots to scavenge DPPH· radical was used as a measure to establish antioxidant activity [32]. Briefly, equal volume of ethanolic solution of DPPH (100 µM) was mixed with separate aqueous solution of genistein and genistein-C-dots in varying concentrations (3.90 µg/ml to 1000 µg/ml). The reaction mixture was incubated for 30 mins under dark conditions. The absorption intensity of the reaction mixture was measured using a Thermo Scientific Evolution 220 UV-Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 518 nm. The DPPH· radical scavenging activity (RSA) was calculated from the following Eq. (1):
(1) RSA (%) = (AControl – ASample)/ AControl X 100
where AControl is the absorbance of DPPH· solution without any genistein or genistein-C-dots at 518 nm; ASample is the absorbance of DPPH· solution with genistein or genistein-C-dots.

Similarly, antioxidant activity of genistein and genistein-C-dots was also measured through their ability to reduce KMnO4 [33]. For this assay, acidic KMnO4 (1 mM) solution (pH=3) was prepared in 1 M H2SO4 and was mixed with aqueous solution of genistein and genistein-C-dots of varying concentrations in equal volume. The reaction mixture was incubated in dark for 1 h followed by measuring absorption intensity at 525 nm. The KMnO4-radical scavenging activity was calculated by using the above Eq. (1).

125 µg/ml genistein-C-dots was prepared in PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.4). Various ROS/RNS species including ClO−, NO, 1O2 and ·OH were prepared freshly at a 3 mM concentration (ROS/RNS solutions were prepared freshly as mentioned below, considering their short lifetime). 2.7 ml of genistein-C-dots solution was mixed with 0.3 ml of ROS/RNS solutions and fluorescence spectra were recorded [34]. All samples were excited at 410 nm (excitation maxima of genistein-C-dots) and intensities of fluorescence emission at 500 nm (emission maxima) were recorded. Intensities of each ROS/RNS were compared to bare genistein-C-dots and presented as percentage of fluorescence intensity. NO : mixture of GSH (0.1844 g) and NaNO2 (0.0414 g) in 0.1 M HCl (10 ml); ClO
−
: NaClO solution was used directly;
1
O
2
: mixing equal concentrations of NaClO and H2O2; ·OH : irradiating H2O2 solution under UV light.

Prior to experiments, RAW 264.7 macrophages obtained from ATCC were grown in DMEM cell culture medium at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Freshly grown cells were collected and counted through hemocytometer to the required numbers.

The cellular uptake of genistein-C-dots was confirmed through confocal microscopy. RAW 264.7 macrophages were grown on sterile glass coverslips inside 12 well flat bottom polysterene plates (Nunc) at a density of 1 × 105 cell per well. Consumed media was replaced with fresh medium constituting genistein-C-dots (62.5 µg/ml) and incubated overnight. Subsequently, cells were washed with sterile PBS for 3 times, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and treated with Hoechst 33258 nuclear stain. The cells were observed by a confocal microscope equipped with an Axiovert-200 M microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) and a Plan-Neofluar 63X/1.4 oil objective excited at 405 nm, 488 nm, and brightfield channel.

Toxicity imparted by genistein-C-dots on RAW 264.7 macrophage cells was measured by the MTT assay. RAW 264.7 macrophages were seeded on 96-well flat bottom polysterene plates (Nunc) at a density of 25,000 cells/well and grown overnight. After 24 h, consumed culture medium was replaced with fresh medium containing genistein-C-dots at different concentrations (3.90 µg/ml to 250 µg/ml) and incubated for 72 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 chamber. Cells treated with only 0.1% DMSO were considered as control. Prior to cellular treatment, genistein-C-dots were dissolved in 0.1% DMSO and filtered with a 0.22 µM syringe filter to avoid contamination. After 72 h the medium was removed, supplemented with 200 µl fresh medium containing 20 µl MTT solution and further incubated for 4 h until formation of formazan crystals. The supernatant was removed and 150 µl DMSO was added to each well in order to solubilize the formazan crystals. Absorbance of solubilized formazan was recorded on a microplate reader at 570 nm. Cell viability was calculated according to the following equation (2):
Cell viability (%) = (absorbance of genistein-C-dots treated groups / absorbance of control group) X 100

For the hemolysis experiment, 5 ml blood samples of 3 male donors were collected from Blood Bank of KIIT Deemed to be University after safety evaluation. Blood samples were diluted with equal volume of sterile PBS. Samples were centrifuged at 1500 g for 5 min and supernatants were discarded to collect RBCs. The RBC suspension was prepared by diluting whole RBCs in sterile PBS. Further, 500 µl of RBC suspension was added to 500 µl genistein-C-dots in PBS, prepared in different concentrations (3.90 µg/ml to 250 µg/ml) in Eppendorf tubes. The mixtures were incubated for 1 h at 37 °C with shaking at 100 rpm. On completion of incubation, the solutions were centrifuged again at 1500 g for 5 min and 100 µl of supernatant from each tube was transferred to a 96-well plate. The absorbance was recorded at 540 nm using a microplate reader. In parallel with genistein-C-dots treatment, RBC suspensions were also treated with 0.1% Triton-X 100 (positive control) and only PBS (negative control). Absorbance values of genistein-C-dots solutions at varying concentration were also recorded to eliminate influence of tested samples on the absorbance intensities. The hemolytic activity was shown as a function of absorbance of released hemoglobin at 540 nm. All experiments including human blood samples were ethically approved from Institutional Ethics Committee, KIIT Deemed to be University (Ref No- KIIT/KIMS/IEC/39–2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effect of genistein-C-dots against ROS/RNS in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Quantification of ROS was carried out by DCFDA cellular ROS detection assay kit following the manufacturer’s instruction. Briefly, 25000 RAW 264.7 macrophages per well were grown in black 96 well plates, stimulated with LPS (100 ng/ml) to induce oxidant stress and treated with different concentrations (15.6 µg/ml to 125 µg/ml) of genistein and genistein-C-dots overnight. Post incubation, cells were treated with DCFDA and fluorescence intensities (emission at 535 nm) were recorded with a microplate reader.

Similarly, reaction with RNS was estimated through quantification of nitric oxide (NO) in RAW 264.7 macrophages by the Griess assay, as previously described [35]. Briefly, 25000 RAW 264.7 macrophages/well were grown on 96-well flat bottom polysterene plates (Nunc), treated with different concentrations (15.6 µg/ml to 125 µg/ml) of genistein or genistein-C-dots with / without LPS (100 ng/ml). After 24-hour incubation, 100 µl of the consumed media was transferred to transparent 96-well plates and mixed with equal volumes of the Griess reagent. The reaction mixture was incubated for 15 min followed by absorbance reading at 540 nm using a microplate reader.

In brief, freshly grown 1 × 105 RAW 264.7 macrophages/ well were treated with genistein or genistein-C-dots with or without LPS (100 ng/ml) overnight. Cell culture supernatants were collected and stored at − 20 °C until further use. Levels of the cytokines TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-10 were measured in cell supernatants with ELISA kits as per recommendation of the manufacturer.

For all assays, at least three independent experiments were performed with appropriate biological/technical replicates and data were expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was calculated through analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by Tukey’s multiple comparison test.

Fig. 1 depicts the experimental outline and characterization of the genistein-C-dots. Fig. 1A illustrates the strategy for synthesis of genistein-C-dots and antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects investigated. Genistein-C-dots were synthesized hydrothermally using a mixture of genistein and ethylenediamine, the latter employed for fluorescence amplification and introduction of amine moieties on the C-dots’ surface [34,36].

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image in Fig. 1B underscores the relative uniform nanoscale size distribution of the genistein-C-dots synthesized; statistical analysis indicates C-dots’ diameters of 6.4 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. S1A & B, supplementary information). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in Fig. 1C (Fig. S2, Supplementary information) confirm that functional moieties of the genistein precursors are retained on the genistein-C-dots’ surface. Specifically, the genistein-C-dots exhibit the flavonoid peak at 1639 cm−1, shifted compared to the corresponding peak in genistein (at 1650 cm−1) due to the presence of the amine residues on the C-dots’ surface [36,37]. In addition, the 1040 cm−1 signals apparent both in genistein and genistein-C-dots account for the C–O–C vibration [37], further confirming the display of genistein-derived functional units on the C-dots. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Fig. S1C, Supplementary information), excitation-dependent emission spectra (Fig. S1D, Supplementary information), and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Fig. S3, Supplementary information) furnish additional characterization of the genistein-C-dots. Genistein-C-dots displayed quantum yield (QY) of about 4%. Fig. 1D depicts water contact angle measurements illuminating the degree of hydrophobicity of the genistein-C-dots. Importantly, genistein-C-dots exhibit a water contact angle 24 ° attesting to a pronounced hydrophilic nature, which stands in contrast to the hydrophobic parent genistein molecules (water contact angle of 125 °). FTIR analysis of genistein-C-dots (Fig. S2, Supplementary information) indicates the presence of hydrophilic groups in genistein-C-dots. XPS data (Fig. S3, Supplementary information) confirm abundance of oxygen and nitrogen containing moieties such as hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine on the surface of the genistein-C-dots. Indeed, hydrophilicity of the genistein-C-dots constitutes an important parameter for efficient solubilization in physiological fluids.

Fig. 2 presents experiments assessing the antioxidant activity of genistein-C-dots. Both the 2,2′-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging assay [32] (Fig. 2A) and KMnO4 assay [33] (Fig. 2B) attest to greater antioxidant activity of genistein-C-dots compared to the parent genistein molecules. Both assays indicate close to 100% scavenging capacity by the genistein-C-dots at a concentration approaching 1000 µg/ml, while the EC-50 value, calculated through non-linear regression analysis, yielded approximately 60 µg/ml for genistein-C-dots (DPPH assay) and 120 µg/ml (KMNO4 assay). These values account for more effective antioxidant activity compared to previously reported flavonoid-C-dots of naringenin (EC50 of 390 µg/ml for DPPH) and red Korean Ginseng (300 µg/ml for DPPH) [29,30]. Hydrophilicity and high positive charge density provided by doped nitrogen on carbon atoms (use of ethylenediamine as N-dopant) causes enhanced interaction between genistein-C-dots and DPPH and KMNO4 attributing to increased antioxidant activity as compared to genistein [38].

The scavenging properties of genistein-C-dots towards prominent ROS and RNS were further determined (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2C depicts the change in fluorescence intensity of the genistein-C-dots after incubation with different ROS (1O2 and ·OH) or RNS (NO). Indeed, significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of genistein-C-dots was recorded upon addition of the C-dots to NO (98%), ClO- (87%), 1O2 (94%) and ·OH (71%). This fluorescence quenching reflects oxidation of surface groups on genistein-C-dots [34,39].

To decipher the underlying physicochemical factors of accounting for the antioxidant properties of genistein-C-dots, we carried out Ultraviolet–Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-DRS) experiments in which reflectance spectra was measured to calculate the band gap energy (Eg). Specifically, the calculated Kubelka-Munk function plots in Fig. 2D indeed reveal that Eg for genistein-C-dots (2 eV) was lower than genistein molecules (3.16 eV). Furthermore, the genistein-C-dots energy bandgap is lower than previously reported plant-based C-dots [40,41], underscoring the excellent antioxidant properties of the C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake and biocompatibility of genistein-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Possible therapeutic applicability of genistein-C-dots depends in the large part on the extent of the C-dots’ uptake by human cells and cytotoxicity of the C-dots. Fig. 3A presents confocal fluorescence microscopy images of RAW 264.7 macrophage cells incubated overnight with genistein-C-dots. Indeed, the microscopy images in Fig. 3A reveal considerable fluorescence staining of the cells indicating efficient cell uptake of the C-dots, likely internalized within the cells through endocytic pathways, accounting for their small size (∼ 6 nm; Fig. 1B) [42,43].

Fig. 3B-C depict cell viability and hemolysis data attesting to the low toxicity of the genistein-C-dots. The effect of genistein-C-dots on cell viability (employing RAW 264.7 cells) was assessed by the MTT assay (Fig. 3B). Indeed, almost 90% cell viability was apparent even at high genistein-C-dot concentrations (up to 250 µg/ml; Fig. 3B). We further tested the degree of hemolysis induced by the genistein-C-dots in human red blood cells (RBCs; Fig. 3C). Hemolysis assessment is important for biosafety evaluation of drug candidates [44]. The hemolysis data in Fig. 3C indicate that genistein-C-dots, employed using the same concentrations as in the MTT assay (e.g., Fig. 3B), did not affect hemoglobin release from the RBCs. The absence of pronounced cell lysis induced by the genistein-C-dots (Fig. 3C & S4, Supplementary information) echoes their low cell toxicity (Fig. 3B), together accounting for their excellent biocompatibility.

As genistein-C-dots were shown to exhibit anti-oxidation properties in controlled “test-tube” experiments (i.e., Fig. 2), we next tested whether the C-dots can mitigate oxidative stress in actual cell environments ( Fig. 4). In the experiments, we stimulated RAW 264.7 cells with lipopolysaccharides (LPS), giving rise to inflammatory conditions [45]. The LPS stimulated cells were subsequently incubated with genistein-C-dots or genistein and the levels of intracellularly secreted radical species were monitored. Specifically, Fig. 4A depicts the intracellular ROS concentrations (e.g., 1O2 and ·OH) via measuring the fluorescence of dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). DCFH-DA is non-fluorescent and transforms into fluorescent 2′− 7′dichlorofluorescein (DCF) upon reacting with ROS. As shown in Fig. 4A, non LPS-stimulated cells exhibit low DCF fluorescence reflecting baseline ROS level; however, the fluorescence intensity significantly increased in LPS-stimulated cells. Importantly, genistein-C-dots reduced intracellular ROS levels in a dose-dependent manner, bringing ROS concentration to baseline level at approximately 60 μg/ml (Fig. 4A). Echoing the anti-oxidation results in Fig. 2, genistein-C-dots were more effective in reducing intracellular ROS levels as compared to genistein itself, attesting to the enhanced functionality of the genistein-C-dots compared to the carbonaceous precursor.

LPS-stimulated cells generally exhibit irregular RNS homeostasis due to upregulation of enzymes such as iNOS and NOS-2, leading to generation and accumulation of intracellular RNS [46–48]. We measured intracellular RNS levels by the Griess assay in which a colorimetric reaction between nitrite and Griess reagent is monitored (Fig. 4B). As shown in the bar diagram in Fig. 4B, cells without LPS stimulation displayed a low nitrite level (baseline), whereas in cells stimulated with LPS the nitrite (RNS) concentration almost quadrupled. However, LPS-stimulated cells incubated with genistein-C-dots featured a dose dependent reduction in RNS levels. Interestingly, RNS levels almost reverted to baseline levels in cells treated with 125 µg/ml genistein-C-dots (Fig. 4B). In both intracellular ROS and RNS assays, the antioxidant effects of the genistein-C-dots (green bars in Fig. 4) were more pronounced than the individual genistein molecules (red bars), further validating the enhanced biological activity of the genistein-C-dots in comparison with the genistein precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of genistein-C-dots on cellular cytokine levels
ce:section.ce:para: Since oxidative stress is responsible for elevation of inflammatory conditions [4], we further examined whether the antioxidant capabilities of genistein-C-dots are also manifested in anti-inflammation effects, particularly in light of the established anti-inflammatory properties of genistein [13]. Fig. 5 depicts the anti-inflammatory impact of genistein-C-dots in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 cells. Specifically, Fig. 5A-B illuminate the levels of the pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 after treatment of the LPS stimulated cells with genistein-C-dots or with genistein molecules alone. Notably, overnight incubation of the LPS-stimulated cells with genistein-C-dots reduced the levels of both TNF-α and IL-6 compared to the untreated cells, as well as in comparison with cells treated with the parent genistein molecules (Fig. 5A-B). Indeed, cells treated with genistein-C-dots at a concentration of 125 µg/ml brought down TNF-α and IL-6 levels to baseline values. Importantly, genistein-C-dots also increased the concentrations of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 in LPS-stimulated cells (Fig. 5C). Like the data recorded for TNF-α and IL-6 (e.g., Fig. 5A-B), genistein-C-dots had a more pronounced effect on intracellular IL-10 level compared to genistein alone (Fig. 5C). All experiments showed that genistein-C-dots are better in regulating the cytokine levels than genistein alone, underscoring the enhanced biological functionalities of genistein-C-dots compared to the parent genistein molecules.

The biophysical and biological results in Figs. 2–5 demonstrate that genistein-C-dots exhibit excellent antioxidant, radical scavenging, and anti-inflammatory properties, in controlled test-tube experiments as well as in vitro. Furthermore, the biological effects of the genistein-C-dots were more pronounced than the parent genistein compound. These observations likely correspond to both the hydrophilicity of the genistein-C-dots, making possible efficient solubilization and cell uptake, as well as the display of abundant genistein-originated functional units on the C-dots’ surface which responsible for the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Notably, genistein-C-dots exhibited pronounced anti-oxidative effects in LPS stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages, attenuated release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6), and increased production of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10. Combined with the low cytotoxicity of the genistein-C-dots, this study underscores the potential of genistein-C-dots in particular, phytochemical-based C-dots in general, as therapeutic vehicles.
We are grateful to the Israel Science Foundation for generous support (grant number 337/20).

Kumar Sagar Jaiswal: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Orit Malka: Methodology, Resources, Formal analysis. Marina Bersudsky: Methodology, Resources, Formal analysis. Elena Voronov: Methodology, Resources, Formal analysis. Nitzan Shauloff: Software, Visualization. Bhawna Gupta: Supervision. Raz Jelinek: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Visualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Kumar Sagar Jaiswal [Ref No. 3/1/2(21)/OBS/2019-NCD–II] is thankful to Indian Council of Medical Research [ICMR], India for financial support and Council of Higher Education (CHE), Israel for infrastructural support.
